resources.string: Signal Insight v1.0.1\n\nAn app for monitoring cellular network signal strength.
Extract colors from wallpaper. Disables custom preset. May not work on all devices
Please grant the following permissions to use the cellular signal monitoring feature
Some permissions have been permanently denied. Please enable them manually in Settings.
Read cell tower and signal strength information
Get dual SIM information (Required for Android 16+)
Associate cell tower with geographic location (select \"Precise Location\")
No SIM card in this slot, cannot switch display
Tap a metric card to view educational explanations and reference standards
The most direct indicator of 4G/5G signal strength. It represents the power level of the signal received from the cell tower.
The closer the value is to 0, the stronger the signal. If the value is too low, you may experience web pages failing to load and choppy calls.
Reflects the \"purity\" of the signal. Even if the signal is strong, too much interference can degrade quality significantly.
Lower values indicate greater environmental interference. This typically occurs in crowded places like train stations or shopping malls.
Signal to Interference plus Noise Ratio (SINR)
The ratio of useful signal to noise. Like listening to someone speak in a noisy room — the higher the ratio, the clearer you can hear.
This is the key metric determining your download speed. Higher SINR means higher 5G speed test scores.
Total power received by the device, including useful signal, interference, and background noise.
In 4G/5G systems, its reference value is generally lower than RSRP.
RSSI measures everything, RSRP measures only the useful signal.
RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator) measures the total power received by your phone, including useful signal, interference, and background noise. It is a broader measurement than RSRP.
If RSSI is much higher than RSRP, there is significant interference in your environment.
If RSSI is close to RSRP, the signal is clean with minimal interference.
Wi-Fi networks: RSSI is the primary signal metric
2G/GSM networks: RSSI is more commonly used
Signal proximity check: good for quick checks
Signal level is within normal range.
Signal is present but may have interference.
Signal is low. Check if you are in an area with good coverage.
A frequency band is a specific range of radio frequencies allocated for wireless communication.
Lower bands penetrate walls better but deliver slower speeds; higher bands like n78 offer extremely fast speeds but poor penetration.
n78/n79 are the most mainstream high-speed 5G bands — extremely fast but with weaker penetration, best used outdoors.
n41 is the主力 5G band, balancing coverage and speed — the core of China\'s 5G network.
B1/B3/B39/B40/B41 are 4G主力 bands, offering broad coverage and balanced speeds — the backbone of mobile networks.
%s is a low-frequency band with wide coverage and strong wall penetration, but relatively slower speeds — ideal for indoor and long-distance use.

A frequency band is a specific range of radio frequencies allocated for wireless communication, like different lanes on a highway. Each band has unique characteristics: higher frequencies offer faster speeds but shorter range and weaker penetration.
Wide coverage, strong penetration. Ideal for rural areas and wide-area deployment.
Balanced coverage and speed. The primary band for urban and suburban areas.
Extremely fast but limited coverage. Best for hotspots and dense urban areas.
%s is a high-speed band. Your 5G experience should be excellent!
%s is a mid-band, balancing speed and coverage. For faster speeds, try moving closer to a 5G tower.
%s is a low band with stable coverage but slower speeds. Best for indoor and long-distance use.
n28 (700M, shared with BRC), n41 (2.6G), n79 (4.9G)
B3 (1800M), B8 (900M), B34 (1900M), B38~B41 (2.6G, partially refarmed)
n41 main 5G band shared with 4G; n28 wide coverage with BRC; n79 for high-capacity hotspots
n1 (2.1G, 4G/5G refarmed), n78 (3.5G, shared with CT)
Shares n78 with China Telecom as main 5G band; n1 refarmed for 4G/5G mixed use
n5 (800M, low-band refarmed), n78 (3.5G, shared with CU)
Co-builds world\'s largest n78 SA network with Unicom; n5 refarmed for low-band 5G coverage
700MHz golden band, extremely wide coverage, leveraging CM\'s existing infrastructure
A unique ID number used to distinguish different cell towers.
When the PCI changes, your phone has completed a cell tower handover.
If PCI changes frequently, you are at the boundary between two cell towers.

PCI (Physical Cell ID) is a unique identifier assigned to each cell sector by the network. It helps your phone distinguish between neighboring cell towers. PCI values range from 0 to 1007 (4G LTE) or 0 to 1007 (5G NR).
Normal handover: PCI changes when you move between cells
When you move from one cell tower coverage area to another, your phone automatically switches to the stronger signal, and the PCI changes accordingly.
If PCI changes rapidly (several times per minute), you are likely at a cell tower boundary or experiencing signal instability.
In 4G LTE networks, PCI Mod 3 should not be the same for neighboring cells, otherwise signal interference occurs. This is why PCI planning is important for network operators.
Current PCI is stable. Your connection is maintaining a consistent cell tower.
PCI has changed. Your phone has handed over to a new cell tower.
Represents the exact frequency position of the cell tower. Used to calculate the precise downlink frequency.
Different operators have different frequency ranges.
EARFCN can be used to calculate the exact frequency in MHz.

EARFCN (E-UTRA Absolute Radio Frequency Channel Number) is a number that represents the exact center frequency of a cell. Network operators use it to identify the specific channel a cell tower is broadcasting on.
Each EARFCN corresponds to a specific downlink frequency in MHz.
China Mobile: B3(1800MHz), B8(900MHz), B39(1900MHz), n41(2.6GHz), n79(4.9GHz)
China Telecom: B1(2100MHz), B3(1800MHz), B5(850MHz), n78(3.5GHz)
Your phone is connected to a specific frequency channel. Different channels within the same band have similar performance characteristics.
Operators divide cell towers into different zones for management.
TAC is updated when you move across zones.
TAC changes less frequently than PCI, usually when you travel to a new area.

TAC (Tracking Area Code) is a number assigned to a group of cell towers in a specific geographic area. Network operators use TAC to manage paging — when someone calls you, the network only pages the cell towers within your current TAC, not the entire network.
Moving to a new district or city center
Subway or train passing through different zones
Flying into a new city (airport arrival)
Efficient paging: Only pages the cell towers in your current area
Location tracking: Helps the network know which region you are in
Load balancing: Prevents the network from wasting resources
Current TAC is stable. Your location area has not changed.
TAC has changed. You have entered a new tracking area.
PCI is available on all networks (2G/3G/4G/5G), named differently per protocol (PCI/PSC/CID)
EARFCN is available on 4G LTE, 5G NR, and 3G WCDMA. 2G GSM does not support this parameter
TAC is used on 4G LTE and 5G NR. 3G WCDMA and 2G GSM use LAC (Location Area Code) instead
RSRP/SS-RSRP is available on 4G LTE and 5G NR. 3G WCDMA and 2G GSM do not support it
RSRQ/SS-RSRQ is available on 4G LTE and 5G NR. 3G WCDMA and 2G GSM do not support it
SINR is available on 4G LTE (via RSSNR) and 5G NR. 3G WCDMA and 2G GSM do not support it

RSSI is available via API on 2G GSM and 4G LTE. 5G NR has RSSI in its protocol (used to compute SS-RSRQ), but Google chose not to expose it to apps due to TDD/beamforming causing unreliable wide fluctuations
Band info is available on 4G LTE and 5G NR. 3G WCDMA and 2G GSM do not support it
About to open browser to visit GitHub repository. Continue?
Controls how often the app polls signal data. Fast (2s) for intensive monitoring, Slow (5s) for battery saving. Actual interval is subject to system scheduling — some devices may not reach 2s
